weeks.title: Week 1 — CORS, Axios wiring + public API endpoints
weeks.description: Connect your React frontend to your live Spring Boot API. Get data flowing end-to-end.

days.label: Day 1 · Monday
days.title: Fix CORS on the Spring Boot side

tasks.text: Learn: what CORS is — browser blocks cross-origin requests unless server explicitly permits them
tasks.description: Understanding CORS explains why your React app's API calls might fail with no obvious error — it's a browser security feature, not a bug in your code.

tasks.text: In SecurityFilterChain: configure CorsConfigurationSource bean
tasks.description: A CorsConfigurationSource bean is how Spring Security applies CORS rules before its filters block cross-origin requests.

tasks.text: Set allowedOrigins to your Vercel URL (and http://localhost:5173 for dev)
tasks.description: Allowing your Vercel and localhost origins is what permits your frontend to actually call this API.

tasks.text: Set allowedMethods: GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE, OPTIONS
tasks.description: Listing allowed methods ensures all the HTTP verbs your frontend uses (including PUT/PATCH/DELETE) aren't blocked.

tasks.text: Set allowedHeaders: * and allowCredentials: true
tasks.description: Allowing all headers and credentials is required for sending the Authorization header and cookies cross-origin.

tasks.text: Test: open browser DevTools → Network tab → confirm no CORS error on OPTIONS preflight
tasks.description: Confirming no CORS error in DevTools is the definitive proof your configuration is correct — error messages alone can be misleading.

days.resource: Common mistake: Adding @CrossOrigin on the controller is not enough when using Spring Security — you must configure CORS inside SecurityFilterChain or it gets blocked by the security filter before reaching the controller.

days.label: Day 2 · Tuesday
days.title: Axios instance + environment variables

tasks.text: In React: create src/api/axiosInstance.js with baseURL from import.meta.env.VITE_API_URL
tasks.description: A shared Axios instance ensures every API call in your app uses the same base URL and config.

tasks.text: Set .env.local: VITE_API_URL=http://localhost:8080/api/v1
tasks.description: Pointing VITE_API_URL at your local backend lets you develop and test against real data.

tasks.text: Add request interceptor: read token from AuthContext (or memory store), attach as Authorization: Bearer {token}
tasks.description: This interceptor is what actually authenticates every outgoing request without repeating code.

tasks.text: Add response interceptor: on 401 response, call logout() and redirect to /login
tasks.description: Auto-handling 401s in one place means session expiry is handled consistently everywhere.

tasks.text: Update all src/api/*.js files to use axiosInstance instead of fetch
tasks.description: Switching from fetch to axiosInstance ensures every request gets the auth header and error handling automatically.

tasks.text: Test: call GET /api/v1/products from React — confirm real data from MySQL appears
tasks.description: Seeing real MySQL data in React confirms your full stack — frontend, backend, and database — is connected end-to-end.

days.resource: Token access tip: Axios interceptors run outside React components, so you can't call useContext() inside them. Store the token in a plain JS module variable (a "token store") that AuthContext writes to on login — the interceptor reads from there.

tasks.text: Update useProducts() hook: queryFn calls real GET /api/v1/products?page=&size=
tasks.description: Swapping mock data for the real endpoint is the moment your frontend becomes a true full-stack app.

tasks.text: Update useProduct(id) hook: queryFn calls real GET /api/v1/products/{id}
tasks.description: The product detail page now reflects real database records instead of placeholder data.

tasks.text: Pass page and size as query params — driven by pagination state in ProductsPage
tasks.description: Driving query params from pagination state keeps the URL, UI, and API request in sync.

tasks.text: Map Spring Boot's Page response object (content, totalPages, totalElements) to the UI
tasks.description: Spring's Page object has a different shape than a plain array — mapping it correctly is required for pagination to work.

tasks.text: Test: browse products on the React app — paginate, click through to detail page
tasks.description: End-to-end browsing and pagination testing confirms the real API integration works for users.

tasks.text: Wire search: GET /api/v1/products/search?name= — update useProducts to accept a search param
tasks.description: Wiring search to the real backend completes the products feature with live data.

tasks.text: Add placeholderData: keepPreviousData to useProducts — switching pages no longer flashes a blank/loading state
tasks.description: keepPreviousData prevents jarring loading flickers when users page through real, network-backed results.

tasks.text: Build useCategories() hook — fetches GET /api/v1/categories
tasks.description: A categories hook follows the same pattern as products — consistency makes the codebase predictable.

tasks.text: Add category filter to ProductsPage — dropdown populated from real categories API
tasks.description: A real category dropdown lets users filter the catalog using actual database categories.

tasks.text: On category select: add categoryId param to products query
tasks.description: Adding categoryId to the query connects the filter UI to the backend's filtering capability.

tasks.text: Add categoryId to queryKey array — ensures cache is separate per category
tasks.description: Including categoryId in the queryKey ensures each category's product list is cached separately.

tasks.text: Test: filter by category — confirm only matching products appear
tasks.description: Testing the filter confirms the frontend and backend agree on how categories are queried.

tasks.text: Handle API errors gracefully — catch Axios errors, extract error.response.data.message
tasks.description: Extracting the backend's error message gives users meaningful feedback instead of a generic failure.

tasks.text: Show user-friendly error messages (not raw JSON) when API calls fail
tasks.description: Friendly error messages are part of a professional user experience — raw JSON looks broken to users.

tasks.text: Confirm skeleton loaders still show correctly while real API fetches
tasks.description: Confirming skeletons still work with real (slower, variable) network calls validates your loading UI under real conditions.

tasks.text: Test with Railway backend offline — confirm error UI shows, not a blank screen
tasks.description: Testing with the backend offline simulates real-world outages — your app should degrade gracefully, not crash.

tasks.text: Add retry logic: useQuery retries 2 times on failure (TanStack Query default is 3 — reduce to 2)
tasks.description: Tuning retry behavior balances resilience against slow failure feedback for users.

weeks.askPrompt: Show me the complete Axios instance setup for a React app — with baseURL from env, a token store module, request interceptor that attaches JWT, and a response interceptor that handles 401 logout

weeks.title: Week 2 — Full JWT auth flow end-to-end
weeks.description: Register, login, token storage, protected routes — all wired to the real Spring Boot auth API.

days.label: Day 1 · Monday
days.title: Register flow — React to Spring Boot

tasks.text: Update useRegister() mutation: POST /api/v1/auth/register with {name, email, password}
tasks.description: Connecting registration to the real endpoint completes the first step of your live auth system.

tasks.text: On success: show success toast, navigate to /login automatically
tasks.description: A success toast and auto-navigation give users clear confirmation and a smooth path to logging in.

tasks.text: On error: catch 409 Conflict (email already exists) — show "Email already registered" message
tasks.description: Handling duplicate email errors specifically gives users a clear reason their registration failed.

tasks.text: On error: catch 400 validation errors from Spring — display field-level messages
tasks.description: Displaying field-level validation errors from Spring mirrors the inline feedback users expect from forms.

tasks.text: Test full flow: register in React → check MySQL Workbench → confirm user row with hashed password
tasks.description: Verifying the hashed password in MySQL confirms registration is secure and working end-to-end.

days.label: Day 2 · Tuesday
days.title: Login flow + token storage

tasks.text: Update useLogin() mutation: POST /api/v1/auth/login with {email, password}
tasks.description: Connecting login to the real endpoint is what actually authenticates users against your database.

tasks.text: On success: extract token and user from response body
tasks.description: Extracting the token and user from the response is the data your app needs to establish a session.

tasks.text: Call AuthContext login() — store token in memory, write user info to localStorage
tasks.description: Storing the token in memory and user info in localStorage balances security with persistence across reloads.

tasks.text: Write token to the token store module (so Axios interceptor can read it)
tasks.description: Writing to the token store lets the Axios interceptor (which can't use React context) access the current token.

tasks.text: Navigate to / (or the page they were trying to reach before being redirected)
tasks.description: Redirecting to the originally intended page after login avoids dropping users back at the homepage unnecessarily.

tasks.text: On error: show "Invalid email or password" — do not reveal which one is wrong
tasks.description: A generic 'invalid credentials' message prevents attackers from learning which emails are registered.

days.resource: Security rule: Never say "email not found" or "wrong password" separately — that lets attackers enumerate valid accounts. Always say "Invalid credentials" for both cases.

days.label: Day 3 · Wednesday
days.title: Session persistence on page refresh

tasks.text: Problem: on page refresh, React state is lost — user appears logged out even with valid session
tasks.description: Recognizing this problem explains why session persistence logic is necessary — without it, every refresh logs users out.

tasks.text: In AuthContext: on mount (useEffect []), read user from localStorage
tasks.description: Reading from localStorage on mount is the first step to restoring a user's session after a refresh.

tasks.text: Call GET /api/v1/auth/me with stored token — verify it's still valid on the server
tasks.description: Verifying the token server-side ensures a stale or revoked token doesn't silently restore an invalid session.

tasks.text: If valid: restore user to state and token to token store — silent re-login
tasks.description: Restoring user state and the token store on success completes the silent re-login.

tasks.text: If invalid/expired: clear localStorage, stay logged out
tasks.description: Clearing storage on an invalid token prevents the app from getting stuck in a broken half-logged-in state.

tasks.text: Add a GET /api/v1/auth/me endpoint in Spring Boot (returns current user from JWT)
tasks.description: /auth/me is the endpoint your frontend depends on to verify sessions — it must exist on the backend.

tasks.text: Test: login, refresh page — confirm user stays logged in
tasks.description: Testing refresh-and-stay-logged-in is the key user experience this whole feature is built for.

tasks.text: Add isAuthLoading state to AuthContext — true while /auth/me is in flight; ProtectedRoute shows a spinner instead of redirecting during this time (prevents a redirect-to-login flash on refresh)
tasks.description: An isAuthLoading flag avoids briefly redirecting logged-in users to /login while their session is still being verified.

days.resource: Add to Spring Boot: @GetMapping("/me") with @AuthenticationPrincipal UserDetails user — returns user details extracted from the JWT by your filter.

tasks.text: Confirm ProtectedRoute redirects to /login for unauthenticated users
tasks.description: Confirming ProtectedRoute still works with real auth data validates your earlier routing work against live data.

tasks.text: Store the originally requested URL: redirect to it after successful login
tasks.description: Remembering the originally requested URL improves UX by returning users to where they wanted to go.

tasks.text: Build AdminRoute: extends ProtectedRoute — also checks user.role === 'ADMIN'
tasks.description: AdminRoute extends the same pattern with a role check — reusing ProtectedRoute avoids duplicating auth logic.

tasks.text: Show "Access denied" page if logged in but not an admin (403 scenario)
tasks.description: An access-denied page handles the case where a logged-in user lacks permission, distinct from not being logged in at all.

tasks.text: In Navbar: show "Admin" link only if user.role === 'ADMIN'
tasks.description: Conditionally showing the Admin link avoids confusing regular users with links they can't use.

tasks.text: Test: login as USER — confirm /admin redirects. Login as ADMIN — confirm access granted
tasks.description: Testing both roles confirms your role-based access control works correctly in both directions.

tasks.text: Update ProductCard / ProductDetailPage: show "Login to add to cart" when AuthContext.user is null, "Add to Cart" when logged in
tasks.description: Prompting logged-out users to log in before adding to cart sets up the checkout flow that requires authentication.

tasks.text: Logout button: call AuthContext logout() — clear state, localStorage, token store
tasks.description: A complete logout must clear every place the session is stored — context, localStorage, and the token store.

tasks.text: After logout: navigate to /login, invalidate all TanStack Query caches (queryClient.clear())
tasks.description: Clearing the query cache on logout prevents the next user from seeing the previous user's cached data.

tasks.text: Token expiry: response interceptor catches 401 → auto-logout + redirect to /login
tasks.description: Auto-logout on 401 ensures expired sessions are handled the same way everywhere in the app, not just on manual logout.

tasks.text: Show "Your session expired, please log in again" toast on auto-logout
tasks.description: A session-expired toast explains to users why they were suddenly logged out, instead of leaving them confused.

tasks.text: Test: manually expire JWT (shorten expiry in Spring Boot to 10s), wait, make API call — confirm auto-logout
tasks.description: Testing with a short-lived token is the only practical way to verify expiry handling without waiting hours.

weeks.askPrompt: Show me how to implement session persistence on page refresh in React — reading from localStorage, calling a /auth/me endpoint to verify the JWT is still valid, then restoring the auth state

weeks.title: Week 3 — Authenticated mutations + file uploads
weeks.description: Wire admin features, image uploads, and user-specific data to your real backend.

days.label: Day 1 · Monday
days.title: Admin product CRUD wired to API

tasks.text: Wire useCreateProduct(): POST /api/v1/products with JWT — admin only
tasks.description: Wiring create to the real API is the first admin feature that actually persists data to your database.

tasks.text: Wire useUpdateProduct(id): PUT /api/v1/products/{id} with JWT
tasks.description: Update lets admins fix mistakes or change product details without deleting and recreating.

tasks.text: Wire useDeleteProduct(id): DELETE /api/v1/products/{id} with JWT
tasks.description: Delete completes the CRUD set — admins can now fully manage the product catalog.

tasks.text: After create/update/delete: invalidate ['products'] queries so list refreshes
tasks.description: Invalidating the products query after any change ensures the list always reflects the latest data.

tasks.text: Build admin product table: list all products with Edit and Delete buttons per row
tasks.description: An admin table with edit/delete actions is the standard interface for managing a collection of records.

tasks.text: Test: create a product as admin in React → verify it appears in MySQL and on product list
tasks.description: Verifying in MySQL and the UI together confirms the full admin CRUD pipeline works correctly.

days.label: Day 2 · Tuesday
days.title: Image upload — React to Spring Boot

tasks.text: Build image upload input in the admin product form — file input, accept="image/*"
tasks.description: A file input is the entry point for the image upload feature you built on the backend in Phase 2.

tasks.text: On file select: show a local preview using URL.createObjectURL(file)
tasks.description: A local preview gives admins immediate visual feedback before the upload completes.

tasks.text: On form submit: construct FormData, append image file and product fields
tasks.description: FormData is the format multipart endpoints expect — combining the image and product fields into one request.

tasks.text: POST FormData to /api/v1/products/{id}/image with Content-Type: multipart/form-data
tasks.description: Posting as multipart/form-data is required for the Spring Boot endpoint to correctly receive the file.

tasks.text: Display returned imageUrl on the ProductCard and ProductDetailPage
tasks.description: Displaying the returned URL closes the loop — uploaded images now appear wherever products are shown.

tasks.text: Test: upload a product image — confirm file saved on server, URL returned, image shows in UI
tasks.description: An end-to-end upload test confirms the file storage, URL generation, and display all work together.

days.resource: Axios tip: When sending FormData, do NOT manually set Content-Type header — let Axios set it automatically with the correct boundary string. Setting it manually breaks multipart parsing.

tasks.text: Build Order entity in Spring Boot: id, user (FK), status, totalAmount, createdAt
tasks.description: The Order entity is the core record of a purchase — everything else this week builds on it.

tasks.text: Build OrderItem entity: id, order (FK), product (FK), quantity, price
tasks.description: OrderItem captures what was bought, how many, and at what price — needed for order history and totals.

tasks.text: Build POST /api/v1/orders — creates order from cart items (authenticated)
tasks.description: Creating an order from cart items is the checkout action that turns a cart into a permanent record.

tasks.text: Build GET /api/v1/orders/my — returns orders for the currently logged-in user only
tasks.description: Scoping orders to the logged-in user ensures customers can only see their own purchase history.

tasks.text: React: build OrderHistoryPage — calls useMyOrders() hook, lists orders with status
tasks.description: OrderHistoryPage gives users visibility into their past orders — a standard e-commerce feature.

tasks.text: React: build "Place Order" button on CartPage — posts cart as an order, clears cart on success
tasks.description: A 'Place Order' button is the final step of the cart-to-order flow your backend now supports.

tasks.text: Spring Boot: GET /api/v1/orders — all orders, admin only, paginated
tasks.description: An admin orders endpoint lets staff see every order across all customers.

tasks.text: Spring Boot: PATCH /api/v1/orders/{id}/status — update order status, admin only
tasks.description: Updating order status is how admins communicate fulfillment progress (e.g. shipped, delivered) to customers.

tasks.text: React: build AdminOrdersPage — table of all orders with status dropdown per row
tasks.description: AdminOrdersPage gives admins a working interface to manage order fulfillment.

tasks.text: On status change: PATCH endpoint, invalidate ['orders'] query on success
tasks.description: Invalidating the orders query after a status change keeps the admin view in sync with the database.

tasks.text: Test full order flow: add to cart → place order → admin changes status → user sees update
tasks.description: An end-to-end order test verifies the full purchase-to-fulfillment flow works for both users and admins.

days.label: Day 5 · Friday
days.title: Optimistic updates + query invalidation patterns

tasks.text: Learn: optimistic update — update UI immediately before server confirms, roll back on error
tasks.description: Optimistic updates make the UI feel instant by updating before the server responds, rolling back only if it fails.

tasks.text: Apply to delete product: remove from UI instantly, restore if DELETE fails
tasks.description: Instant deletion feedback makes the admin table feel responsive even on slower connections.

tasks.text: Apply to order status change: show new status immediately in admin table
tasks.description: Instant status updates in the admin table avoid waiting for a round-trip before seeing the change.

tasks.text: Learn: onMutate, onError, onSettled callbacks in useMutation for optimistic pattern
tasks.description: These callbacks are the building blocks TanStack Query provides specifically for the optimistic update pattern.

tasks.text: Review all mutations — ensure every one invalidates the right query keys on success
tasks.description: A final review of invalidation ensures no part of your app shows stale data after a mutation.

days.resource: Resource: tanstack.com/query/latest/docs/framework/react/guides/optimistic-updates — official guide with complete code example

weeks.askPrompt: Show me how to upload an image file from a React form to a Spring Boot multipart endpoint using Axios and FormData — including the local preview and the backend controller code

weeks.title: Week 4 — Production deployment + end-to-end testing
weeks.description: Deploy both apps, connect them live, and verify the entire system works in production.

days.label: Day 1 · Monday
days.title: Harden Spring Boot for production

tasks.text: Move all secrets to Railway environment variables: DB_URL, DB_USER, DB_PASS, JWT_SECRET, JWT_EXPIRY
tasks.description: Moving secrets to environment variables is the final step in keeping credentials out of your codebase for production.

tasks.text: Set spring.jpa.hibernate.ddl-auto=validate (not update) in production profile
tasks.description: validate (instead of update) prevents Hibernate from silently altering your production schema.

tasks.text: Set CORS allowedOrigins to exact Vercel production URL only (no wildcard *)
tasks.description: Restricting CORS to your exact production URL closes a security gap that a wildcard origin would leave open.

tasks.text: Disable Swagger UI in production: springdoc.swagger-ui.enabled=false
tasks.description: Disabling Swagger in production avoids exposing your full API surface to the public.

tasks.text: Set logging.level.root=WARN in production — reduce log noise
tasks.description: Reducing log verbosity in production avoids noisy, costly logs and potential information leakage.

tasks.text: Push to main branch — confirm Railway auto-deploys and app starts cleanly
tasks.description: A clean production deploy confirms your hardening changes didn't break anything.

tasks.text: Add Spring Boot Actuator (spring-boot-starter-actuator) and expose /actuator/health — set it as Railway's health check URL
tasks.description: A health check endpoint lets Railway automatically detect and restart your app if it becomes unresponsive.

days.resource: Spring profiles: Create application-prod.properties with production overrides. Set SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE=prod in Railway environment variables to activate it.

days.label: Day 2 · Tuesday
days.title: Deploy React to Vercel — production config

tasks.text: In Vercel dashboard: set VITE_API_URL to your Railway production backend URL
tasks.description: Pointing the production frontend at the production backend completes the live connection between both deployments.

tasks.text: Confirm vercel.json has the SPA rewrite rule — all paths route to index.html
tasks.description: The SPA rewrite rule ensures users can refresh or directly visit any route without getting a 404.

tasks.text: Set up a Vercel preview environment: VITE_API_URL pointing to a dev Railway service
tasks.description: A preview environment lets you test changes against a separate backend before they reach production.

tasks.text: Push a change — confirm Vercel builds and deploys automatically from main
tasks.description: Confirming auto-deploy from main verifies your CI/CD pipeline works for ongoing changes.

tasks.text: Run Lighthouse audit on the live Vercel URL — note Performance, Accessibility scores
tasks.description: A Lighthouse audit on the live (not local) site reflects what real users actually experience.

tasks.text: Test on a real mobile device — not just DevTools emulation
tasks.description: Real device testing catches issues (touch targets, viewport quirks) that emulators can miss.

tasks.text: Test USER flow: register → verify email (if implemented) → login → browse products → add to cart → place order → view order history
tasks.description: Testing the full user journey end-to-end is the best way to catch integration bugs that unit tests miss.

tasks.text: Test ADMIN flow: login as admin → create product with image → edit product → change order status
tasks.description: Testing the admin journey ensures the management features work correctly in production, not just locally.

tasks.text: Test session: login → close tab → reopen → confirm still logged in
tasks.description: Testing session persistence across tab closes verifies your auth restoration logic handles real usage patterns.

tasks.text: Test edge cases: checkout with empty cart, access /admin as USER, register with existing email
tasks.description: Edge cases reveal bugs that the happy path won't — testing them now is far cheaper than a user finding them.

tasks.text: Test on multiple browsers: Chrome, Firefox, Safari (mobile) — fix any inconsistencies
tasks.description: Cross-browser testing catches inconsistencies that only appear outside your primary development browser.

tasks.text: Open DevTools → Network tab — look for unnecessary repeated API calls
tasks.description: Spotting redundant API calls in the Network tab is the fastest way to find easy performance wins.

tasks.text: Check TanStack Query devtools — are staleTime values set on frequently-read queries?
tasks.description: Reviewing staleTime settings ensures frequently-read data isn't being refetched more than necessary.

tasks.text: Check image sizes — compress product images, use correct srcset if large
tasks.description: Large uncompressed images are one of the most common causes of slow page loads.

tasks.text: Verify Spring Boot response times in Railway logs — any slow queries?
tasks.description: Checking response times in production logs reveals backend bottlenecks that don't show up locally.

tasks.text: Add MySQL index on commonly filtered columns (e.g. products.category_id, products.name)
tasks.description: Indexes on filtered columns can dramatically speed up queries as your data grows.

tasks.text: Run npm run build locally — confirm bundle size is reasonable (aim under 500kb gzipped)
tasks.description: Checking your bundle size after lazy-loading confirms the code-splitting from Phase 3 is paying off.

tasks.text: Enable show-sql=true locally, call GET /api/v1/products — if you see N+1 queries (one per product), fix with JOIN FETCH or @EntityGraph in the repository
tasks.description: The N+1 problem is one of the most common real-world JPA performance bugs — finding and fixing it here prevents it from surfacing under load.

tasks.text: Add @Cacheable to getAllCategories() in the service — categories rarely change; add @CacheEvict on category create/update/delete
tasks.description: Caching categories avoids repeatedly querying data that almost never changes.

tasks.text: Backend README: architecture diagram, API endpoint table, env vars, local setup steps
tasks.description: A thorough backend README helps reviewers (and future you) understand and run the project quickly.

tasks.text: Frontend README: tech stack, features list, screenshots, live URL, local setup steps
tasks.description: A thorough frontend README does the same for the UI side, including how to see it live.

tasks.text: Take 4-5 screenshots of the live app — product listing, product detail, cart, admin panel
tasks.description: Screenshots give visitors an instant sense of the project without having to run it themselves.

tasks.text: Add screenshots to both READMEs — makes GitHub repos look professional
tasks.description: Embedding screenshots in the README turns a wall of text into an inviting, professional presentation.

tasks.text: Pin both repos on your GitHub profile
tasks.description: Pinning your repos makes your best work the first thing visitors to your GitHub profile see.

tasks.text: Write a short LinkedIn post describing what you built — name the tech stack and link the live app
tasks.description: A LinkedIn post shares your work with your network and can lead to opportunities or feedback.

tasks.text: Export your Postman collection and commit it to the backend repo's /postman folder
tasks.description: A committed Postman collection lets anyone test your live API without writing requests from scratch.

tasks.text: Self-assessment: without notes, explain how a request flows from a React button click to MySQL and back, what happens when a JWT expires, and where CORS is configured
tasks.description: This self-assessment confirms you understand the full request lifecycle across the stack — the core outcome of Phase 4.

weeks.tip: Write down every bug you find on paper. Fix them one by one — don't try to fix multiple things simultaneously or you won't know what fixed what.
weeks.askPrompt: How do I configure Spring Boot application-prod.properties for Railway deployment — with environment variable placeholders for DB credentials, JWT secret, and production-safe JPA settings?

weeks.title: Phase 4 project — live full-stack e-commerce app
weeks.description: The complete system: React on Vercel talking to Spring Boot on Railway over a real MySQL database.

projectCards.heading: What you'll have built
projectCards.text: A deployed, production-grade e-commerce application — users can browse, search, filter, add to cart, register, log in, and place real orders. Admins can manage products (with image upload), categories, and order statuses through a protected dashboard.

projectCards.heading: Architecture
projectCards.text: React (Vercel) → HTTPS → Spring Boot (Railway) → MySQL (Railway) · JWT auth flows through Axios interceptors · Image files stored on Railway filesystem (or Cloudflare R2) · Environment variables manage all credentials

tasks.text: Register and login work end-to-end with real Spring Boot JWT
tasks.description: End-to-end auth proves your frontend and backend security integration works correctly together.

tasks.text: Session persists across page refresh via /auth/me verification
tasks.description: Session persistence is what makes the app feel reliable — users shouldn't be logged out by an accidental refresh.

tasks.text: Expired token auto-logs user out with toast notification
tasks.description: Automatic handling of expired tokens prevents users from hitting confusing failed requests.

tasks.text: Products load from MySQL with real pagination and search
tasks.description: Real pagination and search prove the product browsing experience works against live data at scale.

tasks.text: Admin can create/edit/delete products with image upload
tasks.description: Full admin product management with images is the core content-management capability of the app.

tasks.text: Cart persists in React context, checkout creates real order in DB
tasks.description: A working cart-to-order flow is the critical path of any e-commerce app — it's how the business makes money.

tasks.text: User can view their own order history
tasks.description: Order history gives users confidence and transparency about their past purchases.

tasks.text: Admin can view all orders and update statuses
tasks.description: Order management gives admins the tools to actually fulfill what customers order.

tasks.text: CORS locked to Vercel origin only in production
tasks.description: Locked-down CORS is a basic but essential production security measure.

tasks.text: No secrets in Git — all credentials in environment variables
tasks.description: No secrets in Git is a non-negotiable security baseline for any deployed project.

tasks.text: Both repos have screenshots and setup docs in README
tasks.description: Documentation and screenshots are what make this project usable as a portfolio piece.

tasks.text: Live URL tested on mobile, Chrome, Firefox, Safari
tasks.description: Cross-browser and mobile testing confirms the app works for real users on real devices.

tasks.text: /actuator/health wired as Railway health check, Postman collection committed to repo
tasks.description: Health checks and a Postman collection round out the project as a professional, operable system.

weeks.askPrompt: Help me debug a CORS issue between my React app on Vercel and my Spring Boot API on Railway — what are the most common causes and how do I fix each one?
